title: Why Do I Defend Decisions I Know Are Wrong?

situation.content:
Three months ago the vendor was chosen. Now the cracks are showing, and in the meeting someone gently suggests reconsidering.

The defense comes out — harder than expected. The reasons sounded better in the head. Some part already knows it's wrong. But backing down feels worse than being wrong. It feels like admitting the person who made that call can't be trusted. And that person is me.

story.content:
In 1905, a twenty-six-year-old patent clerk in Bern rewrote the laws of physics. Albert Einstein's papers on relativity and the photoelectric effect didn't just challenge existing science — they dismantled it. He became the man who saw what no one else could see, the physicist whose intuition cut deeper than anyone alive.

Twenty years later, that intuition turned on him.

Quantum mechanics was emerging from the work of Bohr, Heisenberg, and Born — physicists Einstein himself had inspired. Their experiments showed something disturbing: at the subatomic level, nature was fundamentally random. A particle didn't have a definite position until you measured it. The universe, at its smallest scale, ran on probability, not certainty.

Einstein couldn't accept it. "God does not play dice," he told Bohr in a conversation that became famous. It wasn't a casual objection — it was a line in the sand. For the next thirty years, Einstein devised thought experiment after thought experiment trying to prove quantum mechanics was incomplete. Each time, Bohr and his colleagues found the flaw. Each time, the evidence piled higher against Einstein's position.

Here is what makes the story extraordinary: Einstein was not ignorant. He understood quantum mechanics as deeply as anyone alive. He could see the evidence. His close friend Max Born — whose probabilistic interpretation Einstein was rejecting — wrote him letters for decades, gently, persistently. Einstein's replies were warm, affectionate, and unmoved.

He wasn't defending a theory. He was defending the self that had been built on one bedrock conviction: that the universe was, at bottom, elegant, deterministic, and knowable through pure thought. Admitting quantum randomness meant admitting that the deepest intuition of the greatest physicist of the century — the intuition that *was* his identity — had been wrong about the nature of reality itself.

So he spent his final three decades at Princeton, increasingly isolated, working on a unified field theory that never came together, while the rest of physics moved on. Not because he lacked intelligence. Because the cost of changing his mind wasn't intellectual — it was existential.

The man who had once overturned all of physics could not overturn himself.

contrast.content:
Marcus Aurelius, the most powerful man in the Roman world, wrote a private line he never meant anyone to read: "If anyone can prove to me that I am wrong in thought or deed, I will gladly change. I seek the truth, which never harmed anyone."

The emperor with the least reason to listen to correction trained himself to treat it as a gift. He had seen the trap clearly: the higher your standing, the tighter your identity fuses to your choices, and the more it costs to admit one was wrong. So he practiced pulling the two apart on purpose — the decision he made, and the man he was.

principle.content:
We don't defend the decision. We defend the self that made it.

The moment a choice becomes part of identity, changing the mind feels like erasing the self. Separate the two, and the decision becomes just a decision again — something that can be updated without dying.

psychology.content:
Two named mechanisms lock together here.

Cognitive dissonance (Festinger, 1957): when behavior and self-image disagree, the mind feels real discomfort and resolves it the lazy way — by editing reality instead of the self-image. It doesn't feel like rationalizing. It feels like being right.

The second is choice-supportive bias: once a decision is made, memory quietly rewrites it as better-justified than it actually was. Fuse those with identity — "I chose this" becomes "this is who I am" — and any question about the decision registers as an attack on the self, answered with heat instead of reason. Worse, the more intelligent the person, the better a lawyer they make for their own ego.

move.content:
1. Name the real fear in writing. Finish the sentence: 'If I admit this was wrong, people will think I'm ___.' The word is usually incompetent, foolish, or unreliable. Seeing it shrinks it.

2. Split the decider from the decision. State it plainly: 'I made a reasonable call with the information I had. New information changed the picture.' Past-you is not on trial. Present-you is just steering.

3. Change your mind out loud, on purpose. Say 'I was wrong about this, here's what I'm doing instead.' The first time the sky doesn't fall, the spell breaks — and people trust you more, not less.

4. Pre-commit to the off-ramp. When you make a big call, write down in advance: 'I reverse this if I see X.' Now changing course is keeping a promise, not making a confession.

5. Reward the messenger. When someone shows you you're wrong, thank them visibly. You're training the people around you to keep telling you the truth — the only thing that stops you defending the next bad decision even longer.
